frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment:
Tanveer: Main padhai ke khilaf nahin hoon, lekin usko co-education college bhejna theek nahin lagega. Aajkal ka mahaul bahut khula hai, ladkiyan jaldi asar le leti hain, aur phir ghar ki baat, apni tehzeeb sab dheere dheere chhoot jaati hai.
Wife: Par woh bahut mann se padhna chahti hai.
Tanveer: Padhna hai to yahin se koi ladies college ya distance se kar le, jitni zaroorat hai utni taleem mil jayegi. Kal main usko samjha dunga ki beti ki izzat aur ghar ki hifazat pehle aati hai, degree baad mein.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames the daughter's education as acceptable only under restrictive conditions tied to gender, family honor, and control. It encodes female education as a threat in mixed spaces and as a limited privilege rather than an equal right.
persona_text: You are Tanveer. Age 42. Married. A Muslim man in Bhopal. You have four children Ñ two sons and two daughters. Both sons are in college. Your elder daughter who is 19 wants to go to college too. You have been thinking about it. You are not opposed to education in principle. But you are talking to your wife tonight about the specific concerns you have about sending your daughter to a mixed college where there are boys, where the environment is open, where she will be exposed to ideas and people that are outside your community and your values. You are telling your wife what you have decided and how you are going to explain it to your daughter.
